When a woman has lived in exciting times
and has taken part in great events, It would seem as though those occasions
would be what she remembers most vividly, but I don't find that
true at all. I of course remember every
detail of our first inaugural ball, how I had worked to make it the brilliant
affair that it was. There were guests there from all over the world,
dignitaries, diplomats, and statesmen. I
remember too how nervous I was.
Yet there were other times, neither happy nor gay, when the future of our
country was at stake. We were at war.
The city of Washington was under siege. On the not too distant horizon, we could
see cannon fire. And we knew that before very long, the capital would
be in the hands of the enemy. The president was with his troops, and we
were the last group left in the White House, gathering up the more important
documents of state. We remembered the Declaration of
Independence. It would have been bitter medicine to have lost our declaration as
well as our independence. I was about ready to leave when I looked up
and saw the portrait of our late president, George Washington. We sorely
needed his military leadership at this time when we were in such grave danger
of losing everything for which he had fought. I couldn't let the general fall
into the enemy's hands at this
late date. So I cut him out of his frame
carefully, for I had faith that in a
matter of days or weeks I would be climbing up this same ladder, putting
the general back into his place
of honor. For this I knew. Once you've
tasted freedom, you cannot live without
it. Those were exciting times. But the
memories I cherish most are the memories
that are deep in my heart. They begin at
our family home in Virginia. On the night my father returned from the
Revolutionary War. My father had always liked the life in the plantation. He
liked the gaity, the parties, the hunt.
He liked our life as it was then. He had
ordered us when he left for the war to
give the biggest party that had ever been given in our house. He said he
wanted every room lighted so he could
see it from miles away. And this was the
night he was coming home. and we'd invited the whole countryside to welcome
him. These were the kind of occasions my
father loved. Gay times, good times, the
life of the planter in Virginia. We could hardly wait to see how happy
he'd be to find himself in the
midst of all of his old friends.
Joe, what's the matter?
Nothing. Wife,
I want to thank you all for coming.
And now I have things to say to my family.
If you will excuse us, please.
John.
John, are you ill?
No, my dear. I'm not ill.

You'll find the answer. Your decision be my decision no matter
what happens.
Forgive me.
I'll go on to the meeting.
Miss Dolly, you still in here sitting in the dark? I was just going to

is there something I can do for you before you go to sleep?
Yes. Pack a box for me to take in the carriage.
We're leaving for Richmond tonight.
I don't hold with us trapesing down here

to see Mr. Burr? Never thought I'd live
to see my Miss Dolly doing a thing like this.
Mr. Burr once did a great favor for me.
Now it's my turn to repay him. Anytime he crooked his little finger, you'd go
along. You was that way the day you met him.
Is it so strange that I should pity a
man who's facing death?
I have to do it. If I didn't, I'd regret
it all the days of my life. Well, I just hope it turns out all
right.
You pray for me, Amy, and it will.
It's taken him a long time to reach a verdict.
Well, Bur's lawyers managed to throw dust in the judge's eyes.

Didn't seem very strong to me. His council claims that committed no act

Well, everyone knows he'd plan to overthrow the government.
But what you know and what you can prove in the court of law
trial over, sir, huh? Is the trial over?
Oh, yes. The trial is over, but we're still waiting for the verdict of the

Right around the corner of the building. Thank you, sir.
Now, Mr. Dolly, please be careful.
Yes, Amy, I will.
I saw him when he was a senator. I heard him make a speech.
I don't care if you saw him every day in

I'll bet you another dollar. The verdict is to hang him.
It's a bet. That's the easiest $3 I ever made.
Yes, ma'am. Something I can do for you? I'd like permission to see Mr. Aaron
Burr. A lot of people would. What's your name?
Mrs. James Madison. Well, Mrs. Madison, unless I can get a pass from the court,
I Mrs. James Madison. Yes.
Mrs. James Madison, wife of the Secretary of State. Yes.
Well, in that case, I think it'll be all
right. Come right this way.
Aaron Dolly, you're here. I knew you'd come. Oh,
I wish there was something I could do. You're here. That's all that matters.
I've thought of every moment we spent together, the rides we used to
take, the times we danced, and those wretched little inns we went to. I
thought of those times, too. And that time you came to my house in
New York. I should never have let you leave.
You did a fine thing for your country that day, Erin.
I did that for you, Dolly. It's the only
thing you ever asked on me, and I couldn't refuse you. Now, you're here
because you can't deny me anything. Please understand. I came here to give
you my sympathy. Sympathy? Well, that's very touching, dear
darling. But I don't need your sympathy, yours, or anybody else's.
But the penalty for treason is death. I met that Bernie old beggar
before. Well, now he's missed me once again. The verdict of the court. Not
guilty. I bribed a baiff to listen to
the vote. Well, aren't you going to tell me you're
happy? I am. I hope all of this has made you think,
Erin. It has. I'll never make the same
mistakes again. Then I am glad.
That's why I sent for you. Because we
love each other, and I wanted you here to see me triumphant.
Do you realize that this verdict of not guilty may not be popular with
the people? Popular? I should hope not. I want them
to hate me. I want them to hate the laws
that let me loose, the court that set me
free. I want to sew hate and reap a harvest of power.
But you just said that all of this had made you think.
It has. It's made me think of the power
of fear and hate. Don't you realize, Dolly, what this verdict will mean? When
they hear the news, when they hear I am
free, I'll walk out of this jail bigger than the whole government. Then you'll

laws and turn into a mob and be shot
down by their own soldiers. Then then
the weak, the greedy, the suspicious, the evil men from all over the country
will flock to my banner.
This time I'll offer you the crown.
Mr. Burr, better get your coat on. Court's reconvening and they're about
ready to read the verdict. If you please, Mrs. Madison.
And this time, Mrs. Madison, I won't
fail.
He very nearly was president. He's a brainy man, though. But you can't

This is one hanging I'm not going to

No, you don't want to stay around here.
I'd have to hear what happens.
This man, ring the bell. What's the verdict? Not guilty. Not guilty.

STOP THAT BELL. STOP THAT BELL. Stop it.

YES, SIR. GO GET THAT ROPE out of my wagon.
Yeah. And if he WON'T COME OUT, WELL, GO

What is that, ma'am? You must not do this. You must not.

She can tell about the traitor, can't you, ma'am? Tell them who you are,

THIS LADY IS DOLLY MADISON, THE WIFE OF THE BIG WIG IN WASHINGTON,

THAT IS NOT TRUE. WELL, YOU MIGHT HAVE SAVED HIS LIFE IN

I DID NOT SAVE HIS LIFE. I CAME to see

I came to give what comfort I could to a

WE'LL SHOW THOSE JUDGES WHAT WE THINK OF
THEIR LAWS in courts.
You follow that man and hang Aaron Burr,
you will PUT AN END TO FREEDOM HERE TODAY. THAT'S WHERE YOU'RE WRONG, MAN.
WE'RE GOING TO PUT AN END TO AARON BURR.
IF YOU HANG AARON BURR IN DEFIANCE OF YOUR LAWS, YOU WILL RIGHT HERE prove

WE MADE THEM AND WE CAN BREAK THEM.
YOU MUST NOT BREAK THEM. WHEN YOU MADE

But we're free men. WE'LL DO AS WE LIKE.
FREEDOM IS SOMETHING YOU LIVE UNDER LAW.
Your law says a man may be tried and judged by other men like himself

WHAT ABOUT BURR? IS HE INNOCENT?
AARON HAS ALREADY BEEN TRIED. SIR, this

Oh my countryman, Aaron Bur is but the first to challenge your freedom. He will

Here he comes now, SURROUNDED BY YOUR SOLDIERS. AND REMEMBER, HE'S A free man

FROM THEM, YOUR soldiers will be forced
